title: ASML raises 2026 revenue outlook to €43–45B, second guidance hike this year on AI demand
subtitle: The Dutch lithography monopoly beat Q2 estimates, is adding 30 percent more EUV and DUV capacity, and is now cutting tool build-and-test time by roughly a third to keep up.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: ASML, the Dutch company that makes the extreme-ultraviolet lithography machines every leading-edge chip fab depends on, raised its 2026 revenue guidance on July 15 to a range of €43 billion to €45 billion, up from €36–40 billion — the second increase this year. Q2 net sales came in at €9.3 billion against a €8.8 billion consensus, gross margin hit 54 percent, and CEO Christophe Fouquet described first-half order intake as 'extremely strong.' Q3 guidance was set at €11–12 billion. Full-year memory-related revenue is now projected to grow more than 75 percent, driven by HBM and DDR capacity expansions.

The mechanism behind the guidance raise is worth naming. ASML sells the tool. TSMC, Samsung, and SK Hynix buy it to build fabs for Nvidia, Anthropic, Meta, OpenAI, and every other AI capex plan announced this year. When ASML raises guidance twice in six months, that is downstream demand hardening into pre-committed capital, not a forecast — the machines take years to build, ship, and install. The company also said it is shortening its tool build-and-test time by about a third to move faster, which is what a supplier does when demand exceeds its ability to deliver.

This continues a pattern the feed has covered for weeks: TSMC's N3 line sold out through year-end, Samsung and SK Hynix riding an AI memory supercycle, TSMC's June revenue up 68 percent year-over-year, and Blackstone dropping thirty billion on Japanese AI data centers. All of it is downstream of the same physical bottleneck. ASML is the narrowest point in the AI supply chain — a single company, in a single country, whose EUV export licenses to China are a live geopolitical file. If that node twitches, every AI roadmap on the planet slips.

A note for learners: the AI capex story is easier to reason about if you learn the stack from the bottom up. ASML makes the machine that etches the transistors. TSMC etches the wafer. Nvidia and Broadcom package the die. Meta and OpenAI rent the finished chip. When any layer above ASML raises money, the money eventually flows down to a queue slot on a Dutch lithography tool. Knowing that order-of-operations is more useful than tracking any single model release, because it tells you where the real constraint sits — and that constraint moves slowly.
